summary: One of the largest known theropods. Giganotosaurus carolinii embryos represent a high-value genetic asset with documented pack-hunting behavior in the wild.
facts: Skull and dentition optimized for slicing — less crush, more cleave.
Fossil evidence from Argentina supports multi-individual kills.
InGen telemetry: coordinated flanking when prey is cornered.
Embryo batch among the most genetically complete carnivore lines on file.
fieldNotes: Assume multiple individuals in operational zone. Extract embryos before engagement.
